title: Do monitoring or advisory lead independent directors with financial expertise matter to managerial opportunism?
abstract: Motivated by the increasing interest and the monitoring and advisory roles that a lead independent director may play to decrease managerial opportunism; we examine the relationship between monitoring or advisory lead independent directors and managerial opportunism proxied by earnings management. Using a panel data of US firms from 2001 to 2020, we find that monitoring lead independent directors rather than advisory lead independent directors is negative and significantly associated with discretionary accruals. In an additional analysis, we find that advisory rather than monitoring lead independent directors improve earnings persistence and future cash flows, suggesting that both monitoring and advisory lead independent directors play complementary roles when it comes to corporate performance/earnings quality. We also find that, regardless of the role they play within boardrooms, monitoring and advisory lead independent directors with financial expertise have a decreasing effect on discretionary accruals. These findings provide new insights and improve our understanding of how monitoring and advisory lead independent directors contribute to corporate performance in complementary ways.
text:
Do monitoring or advisory lead independent directors with financial 
expertise matter to managerial opportunism?
Andrews Owusu a,*
, Wansu Hu b , Frank Kwabi b , Kamil Omoteso c
a Derby International Business School, University of Derby, Kedleston Road, Derby DE22 1GB, UK 
b Leicester Castle Business School, De Montfort University, Leicester LE1 9BH, UK
c Faculty of Business and Law, Anglia Ruskin University, East Road, Cambridge CB1 1PT, UK
A R T I C L E I N F O
Keywords:
Lead independent directors 
Monitoring and advisory roles 
Earnings management
A B S T R A C T
Motivated by the increasing interest and the monitoring and advisory roles that a lead independent director may 
play to decrease managerial opportunism; we examine the relationship between monitoring or advisory lead 
independent directors and managerial opportunism proxied by earnings management. Using a panel data of US 
firms from 2001 to 2020, we find that monitoring lead independent directors rather than advisory lead inde-
pendent directors is negative and significantly associated with discretionary accruals. In an additional analysis, 
we find that advisory rather than monitoring lead independent directors improve earnings persistence and future 
cash flows, suggesting that both monitoring and advisory lead independent directors play complementary roles 
when it comes to corporate performance/earnings quality. We also find that, regardless of the role they play 
within boardrooms, monitoring and advisory lead independent directors with financial expertise have a 
decreasing effect on discretionary accruals. These findings provide new insights and improve our understanding 
of how monitoring and advisory lead independent directors contribute to corporate performance in comple-
mentary ways.
1. Introduction
With the ongoing discussions on board leadership structure and the 
pressure from corporate watchdogs and legislators to separate the Chief 
Executive Officer (CEO) and the board chair positions, regulators are 
facing pressure to implement a lead independent director policy. The 
appointment of a lead independent director extends beyond addressing 
the concerns associated with the combined role of the CEO and the 
board chair (CEO duality). For instance, while the United States (US) 
regulators in an attempt to resolve the concerns raised by corporate 
watchdogs and legislators, moved to implement a lead independent di-
rector policy to avoid the separation of the CEO and the board chair 
positions (Dalton & Dalton, 2005; Krause et al., 2017), regulators in the 
European countries including France, Germany, Italy and the United 
Kingdom (UK), where CEO duality is rare, have also implemented 
similar policy (Davies & Hopt, 2013; Allianz Global Investors, 2022). 
Nevertheless, these regulators have not made it clear whether lead in-
dependent directors should play one of the two main documented board
of directors’ roles – monitoring or advisory (Adams & Ferreira, 2007; 
Faleye et al., 2013; Hsu & Hu, 2016) 1 − or whether it is sufficient for 
these directors to merely add to the board leadership structure. 
According to Krause et al. (2017), a lead independent director is 
appointed to counterbalance the CEO’s power. Indeed, academics and 
regulators contend that a lead independent director representation on 
the board (i) should be considered as a credible alternative when CEO 
duality is visible, (ii) provides a sounding board for the chair and serves 
as a go-between the remaining directors and shareholders, (iii) attends 
to the concerns and builds consensus when there is a dispute between 
the CEO and the board chair, and (iv) ensures investors’ concerns that 
have not been addressed by the board are resolved (Owusu et al., 2023). 
Consequently, a considerable number of studies have documented the 
positive impact of a lead independent director on multiple corporate 
outcomes including corporate governance quality, firm performance, 
managerial risk-taking, and cost of debt (Krause et al., 2017; Lamoreaux 
et al., 2019; Rajkovic, 2020; Owusu et al., 2023). More pertinent to our 
study, though, is that Bryan and Mason (2023) and Hsu et al. (2024)

show that lead independent directors’ representation on the board has 
the propensity to reduce managerial opportunism associated with 
earnings management, and this is especially so when they serve on the 
audit committee, become older, serve longer on the board and when 
they have a higher educational background (Ph.D.). Their evidence, 
however, is in sharp contrast to Larcker et al. (2007) who find no as-
sociation between a lead independent director representation on the 
board and earnings management.
Nevertheless, existing regulators and academic researchers precisely 
focused on the mere addition of a lead independent director to the board 
leadership structure without considering the channels through which a 
lead independent director may contribute to the board leadership 
effectiveness. For instance, the mere appointment of a lead independent 
director appears to be a symbolic management tactic to satisfy regula-
tors with less effectual (Shi & Connelly, 2018). However, a lead inde-
pendent director policy has been influenced by the concerns raised by 
corporate watchdogs and legislators without being informed by reliable 
evidence associated with their potential impact on board leadership 
effectiveness (Dalton & Dalton, 2005). In terms of the board leadership 
roles, it is not empirically clear in existing literature whether the 
monitoring role or advisory role is most suitable for lead independent 
directors. So far, no study has gone beyond the mere participation of 
lead independent directors on corporate boards and examined how these 
directors' monitoring or advisory roles affect earnings management. Our 
study fills this gap in the existing literature by examining the differential 
effect of monitoring or advisory lead independent directors on earnings 
management.
Additionally, prior research (e.g., Bryan & Mason, 2023; Hsu et al., 
2024) aimed at exploring the underlying mechanisms through which the 
mere participation of lead independent directors on the board affects 
earnings management has focused on their experience and expertise 
such as audit committee membership, age, tenure and educational 
background with insufficient attention to their financial expertise. 
Nevertheless, to use their capacity as better monitors or advisors to 
mitigate the financial reporting decisions that violate the underlying 
accounting principles, lead independent directors may need to have a 
better understanding of the financial reporting process, and therefore 
their financial knowledge is crucial. Our study fills this gap in the 
existing literature by examining the role of financial expertise in the 
relationship between monitoring or advisory lead independent directors 
and earnings management.
There are several reasons that a lead independent director moni-
toring role rather than an advisory role can enhance corporate gover-
nance and constrain earnings management. First, in common with the 
compromise board leadership structure theory which assumes that the 
appointment of a lead independent director allows managerial auton-
omy with the required monitoring (Krause et al., 2017), we argue that 
the monitoring rather than the advisory role of a lead independent di-
rector is likely to exert influences on managers to adopt financial 
reporting decisions in favour of less earnings management. Second, 
unlike the lead independent directors in an advisory role, the member-
ship in monitoring committees (e.g., audit committee) allows lead in-
dependent directors in a monitoring role to have direct interaction with 
the external audit firm and challenge a powerful CEO and managers to 
address accounting issues raised by the committees and the external 
auditors. Therefore, one can argue that, because of lead independent 
directors’ unique responsibilities and power, their monitoring rather 
than advisory role is more likely to play a crucial role when it comes to 
the quality of financial reporting. Third, prior literature documents that 
the opportunistic behaviour of managers can be controlled through 
monitoring (see Wild, 1996; Dechow et al., 1996), so a lead independent 
director in a monitoring role than an advisory role can improve the 
credibility, integrity, and reliability of financial information. We, thus, 
expect a lead independent director in a monitoring, rather than an 
advisory role, to be associated with a decrease in earnings management. 
To test our prediction, we use discretionary accruals as a measure of
managerial opportunism. Our empirical analysis employs a panel data-
set consisting of 6,664 US firm–year observations between 2001 and 
2020. We find strong evidence to support our prediction that a lead 
independent director in a monitoring role, rather than in an advisory 
role, is negatively and significantly associated with discretionary ac-
cruals. Our findings continue to hold after using a number of robustness 
tests including alternative measures of discretionary accruals, moni-
toring and advisory lead independent directors, and several techniques 
to control for endogeneity.
Next, we examine whether a lead independent director’s monitoring 
and advisory roles may complement each other when it comes to 
corporate performance. While Hsu and Hu (2016) demonstrate that in-
dependent directors’ advisory role rather than their monitoring role is 
more effective in increasing earnings persistence, our findings suggest 
that a lead independent director monitoring role rather than an advisory 
role reduces abnormal accruals. These contrasting findings may be due 
to the difference in earnings quality measures used and calls for further 
tests to explore whether lead independent directors’ monitoring and 
advisory roles contribute to corporate performance in complementary 
ways. We show that firms with a lead independent director monitoring 
role are less likely to benefit from persistent earnings quality and higher 
future cash flow than those firms with a lead independent director in an 
advisory role. These results suggest that lead independent directors’ 
monitoring and advisory roles contribute to corporate performance in 
complementary ways.
In addition, we assess the underlying mechanism through which a 
lead independent director may use to reduce discretionary accruals in 
their monitoring or advisory roles. Specifically, we explore how finan-
cial expertise moderates the relationship between a lead independent 
director monitoring or advisory roles and earnings management. We 
show significant results that lead independent directors in monitoring or 
advisory roles with financial expertise can restrain managerial oppor-
tunism toward discretionary accruals. Our findings suggest that lead 
independent directors with financial expertise have the propensity to 
decrease earnings management regardless of the role that they play 
within the boardroom.
Our study advances the literature in two important aspects. First, we 
expand the literature on the relationship between lead independent di-
rectors and earnings management (Larcker et al., 2007; Bryan & Mason, 
2023; Hsu et al., 2024) by analysing whether the monitoring or advisory 
lead independent director plays any role in the relationship. In doing so, 
we place each lead independent director in a specific role and treat the 
monitoring role than advisory role as the key determinant of earnings 
quality. Our novel approach draws on the two documented monitoring 
and advisory board roles (Adams & Ferreira, 2007; Faleye et al., 2011). 
These differentiated board roles form the basis for our discussion and 
interpretation of the results, enabling us to advance the line of research 
examining the novel contribution of the mere presence of a lead inde-
pendent director on the board to a lead independent director role-
earnings management relationship.
Second, we advance the literature on the relationship between the 
board of directors and earnings persistence (Hsu & Hu, 2016) by 
showing how monitoring and advisory lead independent directors play 
complementary roles. Unlike Hsu and Hu (2016) who find that advisory 
than monitoring independent directors increase earnings persistence, 
we reveal that while monitoring lead independent director role reduces 
abnormal accruals, their advisory role increases earnings persistence. 
These findings have positive implications for firms. To ensure strong 
oversight, accountability, and compliance, especially when it comes to 
management discipline around financial reporting quality, it is impor-
tant for firms to appoint a lead independent director to a monitoring 
role, while in the areas of strategic insight and persistent earnings, an 
advisory lead independent director is suitable. We conclude that firms 
can benefit from earnings quality (earnings persistence) when they 
appoint lead independent directors and assign them to the monitoring 
role (advisory role).

The rest of the paper is organised as follows. The next section dis-
cusses the related literature and hypothesis development. Section 3 
presents data and explains how earnings management is constructed. 
Section 4 provides the empirical analysis. Section 5 concludes the paper.
2. Related literature and hypotheses
2.1. Theoretical framework
A number of theories, including agency, stewardship or classical 
organisation, and compromise board leadership structure theories, can 
broadly be used to predict the association between lead independent 
director roles and earnings management. For example, agency theory 
has long been used to study board leadership structure (e.g., Berle & 
Means, 1932; Jensen & Meckling, 1976). This theory postulates that the 
inherent conflict of interests between managers and outside share-
holders has long been established (e.g., Berle & Means, 1932) and, as a 
result, a board leadership structure with a combined CEO and the board 
chair positions has the propensity to empower one individual to take an 
entrenched position and increase agency costs (Fama & Jensen, 1983). 
Therefore, role separation between the CEO and the board chair posi-
tions arguably allows for checks and balances with the possibility of 
constraining managerial opportunism. Fama (1980) and Jensen and 
Meckling (1976), however, argue that because managers are anxious 
about their reputation and employment risk, they become risk-averse, 
and thus independent monitoring alongside incentives is needed to 
encourage managerial risk-taking to influence their underlying financial 
performance (Baysinger & Hoskisson, 1990; Jensen & Murphy, 1990). 
These arguments lead to the possibility that the monitoring and advisory 
roles of lead independent directors may be needed to increase mana-
gerial risk-taking to boost the underlying financial performance but 
managers’ tendencies toward risk-taking are likely to encourage them to 
engage in opportunistic incentives driving behaviour such as accruals 
earnings management.
Stewardship or classical organisation theory, however, argues that a 
board leadership structure with the combined CEO and board chair 
positions brings about the unity of command and clear lines of re-
sponsibility, thereby leading to quick decision-making (Fayol, 1949; 
Pfeffer, 1981; Dalton et al., 1998). The combined CEO and board chair 
positions arguably align managers’ incentives with the objectives of the 
firm, and thus managers are less motivated to engage in economic pri-
vate interests. Donaldson and Davis (1991; 1994) argue that these 
managers possess firm-specific knowledge and expertise necessary to 
implement suitable accounting policies that are likely to mitigate ac-
cruals earnings management and encourage them to provide accurate 
financial information for investors. These arguments suggest that a 
board leadership structure with a combined CEO and board chair posi-
tions is less likely to engage in managerial opportunism and, as a result, 
the monitoring and advisory roles of lead independent directors are less 
likely to make a difference.
In an attempt to reconcile the contrasting standpoints of agency 
theory and stewardship or classical organisation theory, Krause et al.
(2017) developed the compromise board leadership structure theory, 
which believes that board leadership structure in the presence of a lead 
independent director provides a compromise solution to managerial 
autonomy and the necessary monitoring. They argue that the appoint-
ment of a lead independent director provides a balanced power, espe-
cially when the CEO and the board chair positions are occupied by one 
individual thereby constraining managerial opportunism. On the other 
hand, if there is a role separation between the CEO and the board chair 
positions but the CEO power is not sufficiently tempered by an inde-
pendent board chair then the presence of a lead independent director is 
more likely to mitigate the opportunistic incentives driving behaviour. 
These arguments suggest that the monitoring and advisory roles of lead 
independent directors may be effective in mitigating managerial 
opportunism when there is a combined CEO and board chair position or
role separation between the two, thereby constraining accruals earnings 
management. In summary, existing board leadership structure theory 
provides competing predictions on how the monitoring and advisory 
roles of lead independent directors should impact managerial 
opportunism.
2.2. Empirical research
Several studies have attempted to analyse the relationship between a 
lead independent director's representation on the board and different 
corporate outcomes, but the results are less clear. Chen and Ma (2017) 
find that CEO dismissal due to poor firm stock performance is sensitive 
to the presence of a lead independent director. Krause et al. (2017) find 
that under the right conditions, the presence of a lead independent di-
rector improves firm performance, especially through a balanced power 
that is reflected in the board leadership structure. Lamoreaux et al.
(2019) find that the appointment of a lead independent director is not 
window dressing because their presence on the board improves firm 
value and corporate governance quality. Rajkovic (2020) finds that a 
lead independent director representation on the board is more sensitive 
to investment efficiency and future firm performance. Owusu et al. 
(2023) report that the presence of a lead independent director decreases 
managerial risk-taking and the corresponding cost of debt. These find-
ings are consistent with lead independent directors providing a 
compromise solution to managerial autonomy and the necessary 
monitoring to improve corporate outcomes. However, Shi and Connelly
(2018) argued and provided evidence to show that the appointment of a 
lead independent director is a symbolic management tactic to satisfy 
regulators. They find that firms listed on the New York Stock Exchange 
(NYSE) compared to those listed on the National Association of Secu-
rities Dealers Automated Quotations (NASDAQ) appoint lead indepen-
dent directors to deceive transient institutional investors than dedicated 
institutional investors because their appointment to meet the NYSE 
listing requirement relative to the organic adoption under the NASDAQ 
is less effectual.
While the foregoing empirical research is useful in terms of broadly 
highlighting the value of a lead independent director representation on 
the board, several important questions remain unresolved. First, existing 
research has mainly analysed the mere representation of lead indepen-
dent directors on corporate boards and disregarded the differential ef-
fect of the two most important roles (i.e., monitoring and advisory) that 
each director on the board should perform. This distinction is extremely 
important because extant research (e.g., Jensen, 1993; Adams & Fer-
reira, 2007; Faleye et al., 2011, 2013; Hsu & Hu, 2016) suggests that 
corporate boards perform two main roles – monitoring and advisory – 
and that independent directors tend to be more involved in one instead 
of the two main roles due to the limited time available to them. Second, 
whereas in the early 2000 s regulators in the US introduced the 
appointment of a lead independent director to avoid role separation 
between the CEO and the board chair (e.g., Dalton & Dalton, 2005; 
Krause et al., 2017), it is uncertain whether these directors are appointed 
to perform monitoring or advisory roles that can be potentially more 
beneficial to shareholders. Alternatively, firms might be motivated to 
appoint these directors to perform non-strategic roles that are detri-
mental to shareholders to satisfy regulatory requirements.
Third, given that a lead independent director provides a compromise 
solution to ensure a balanced power is reflected in the board leadership 
structure (Krause et al. 2017); it is surprising that their impact on 
managerial opportunism measured in terms of earnings management 
has received limited attention. An exception to this is a recent study by 
Bryan and Mason (2023) and Hsu et al. (2024) who report that firms 
with a lead independent director representation on the board engage in 
less earnings management, but their evidence is in sharp contrast with 
an earlier work by Larcker et al. (2007) that report no association be-
tween a lead independent director and earnings management. While 
Larcker et al. (2007), Bryan and Mason (2023) and Hsu et al. (2024)

studies provide useful insights into the value of a lead independent di-
rector in constraining managerial opportunism, they simply focused on 
the mere participation of lead independent directors and disregarded the 
differential effect of these directors' main roles that they should play. 
Therefore, we seek to contribute to the existing literature by analysing 
the differential effect of lead independent directors’ monitoring and 
advisory roles on managerial opportunism.
2.3. Hypotheses
The theoretical discussion above provides competing predictions on 
how a lead independent director's monitoring and advisory roles impact 
managerial opportunism and suggests the need to analyse the differen-
tial effect of lead independent directors’ roles on accruals earnings 
management. The preceding empirical research broadly suggests that 
the mere participation of a lead independent director is related to a 
number of corporate outcomes. In appointing lead independent di-
rectors, corporate boards are expected to assign them to one of the two 
main board roles − monitoring and advisory. While independent di-
rectors who serve on audit, governance, nomination, and compensation 
committees are considered as undertaking monitoring roles, those who 
serve on strategy, investment, and finance committees engage in advi-
sory roles (Faleye et al., 2011, 2013; Kim et al., 2014). Considering that 
the monitoring committees are dominated by independent directors 
who spend more time undertaking their monitoring responsibilities than 
the executive-dominated advisory committees (Heidrick & Struggles, 
2007; Adams, 2009; Hsu & Hu, 2016), one can argue that lead inde-
pendent directors in monitoring rather than advisory roles have the 
propensity to constrain managerial opportunism. This expectation is 
consistent with the compromise board leadership structure theory 
argument that the presence of a lead independent director provides a 
compromise solution to managerial autonomy and the necessary 
monitoring (Krause et al., 2017).
In addition, as the membership in monitoring committees allows 
lead independent directors in a monitoring role to interact with the 
external audit firm and the CEO when it comes to the financial reporting 
decisions compared with lead independent directors in an advisory role, 
we argue that this direct interaction between them is more likely to 
address financial reporting issues, suggestions, and concerns raised by 
the committees and the external auditors. An interesting parallel argu-
ment may be made here that their unique position in the board lead-
ership structure suggests that lead independent directors when serving 
on monitoring committees are likely to strengthen the committees' 
power, improve the oversight of the financial reporting process, and 
effectively and efficiently mediate financial reporting issues between 
managers and external auditors. If firms with lead independent directors 
engage in less accruals earnings management as reported by Bryan and 
Mason (2023) and Hsu et al. (2024), then we would expect lead inde-
pendent directors in monitoring rather than advisory roles to constrain 
managerial opportunism measured in terms of accruals earnings man-
agement. This leads us to our first hypothesis in an alternative form as 
follows:
H1: Accruals earnings management is lower for firms with a lead inde-
pendent director in a monitoring role rather than in an advisory role.
Next, we attempt to examine whether our predicted relation between 
monitoring and advisory lead independent directors and accruals 
earnings management is sensitive to their specialist skills and profes-
sional expertise, and in our particular case, their financial expertise. This 
is important because extant studies (i.e., Xie et al., 2003; Abbott et al., 
2004; Bedard & Johnstone, 2004; Dhaliwal et al., 2010; Badolato et al., 
2014) suggest that independent directors with a financial background 
have the propensity to constrain earnings management. However, these 
studies have not always analysed how lead independent directors 
distinctive from regular independent directors with a financial back-
ground are related to earnings management, particularly in the case of
monitoring and advisory lead independent directors, which suggests a 
very important limitation. This is particularly so in the context of the 
argument that directors can be efficient monitors of managers if they 
have monitoring incentives and understand the ramifications of finan-
cial reporting decisions (Beekes et al., 2004).
Although Bryan and Mason (2023) and Hsu et al. (2024) find that the 
presence of lead independent directors conditioned by their experience 
and expertise (i.e., audit committee membership, age, tenure and higher 
educational background) is strongly associated with lower accruals 
earnings management than when not conditioning, they have not paid 
sufficient attention to how lead independent directors in the monitoring 
and advisory roles with financial expertise that is distinct from regular 
independent directors affects earnings management. We argue that the 
monitoring rather than advisory lead independent directors’ ability to 
constrain earnings management largely depends on their monitoring 
incentives and a better understanding of the financial reporting process 
and the underlying accounting principles. Unlike regular independent 
directors who have less power and authority to challenge the CEO and 
managers, lead independent directors with additional leadership re-
sponsibilities among independent directors are better placed to have 
regular interaction with the officers and present any financial reporting 
issues raised by regular independent directors and auditors to be cor-
rected, especially when they have financial expertise. Therefore, the 
financial expertise of lead independent directors is likely to strengthen 
their monitoring abilities to improve the quality of financial reporting. 
As discussed, and hypothesise above, we would expect accruals 
earnings management to be lower for firms with a lead independent 
director in a monitoring role rather than in an advisory role. If moni-
toring than advisory lead independent director decreases accruals 
earnings management, then we would expect this result to be more 
prominent in firms that appoint monitoring rather than advisory lead 
independent directors with financial expertise. This leads us to our 
second hypothesis in an alternative form as follows:
H2: The negative relation between monitoring than advisory lead inde-
pendent directors and accruals earnings management is stronger for firms 
that appoint lead independent directors with financial expertise.
3. Sample, data and variable definitions
3.1. Sample and data
We construct our sample by first collecting monitoring and advisory 
data from the BoardEx database, where the monitoring and advisory 
committees that the lead independent directors serve on, and other 
board-related variables are made available. Considering that the lead 
independent director’s role became prominent in the early 2000 s, we 
begin constructing our sample from 2001. We also collect financial data 
required to calculate our dependent and control variables from the 
Thomson Reuters Worldscope database. We then merge the two distinct 
datasets and undertake the following procedures. First, we delete firm-
year observations with missing data to calculate our independent, 
dependent and control variables. Second, we delete financial sector 
firms from our sample due to their distinct financial reporting and reg-
ulatory environment. Third, we delete industries with less than 20 
industry-year observations in order to have sufficient data to calculate 
our dependent variable (i.e., earnings management). Finally, we win-
zorise all continuous variables at the 1st and 99th percentiles to reduce 
the potential outlier concerns. We obtain a total number of 6,664 firm-
year observations over the period 2001–2020 after the preceding pro-
cedures to undertake our empirical analysis.

3.3. Proxy for monitoring and advisory lead independent directors
In identifying monitoring and advisory lead independent directors, 
we first follow previous literature (e.g., Bryan & Mason, 2023; Hsu et al., 
2024; Owusu et al., 2025) and categorise each director as a lead inde-
pendent director if the role title in BoardEx is lead director, lead inde-
pendent director, lead outside director, presiding director, facilitating 
director, lead trustee, lead independent trustee or presiding trustee. We 
then measure monitoring and advisory lead independent directors 
following Faleye et al. (2011). Specifically, we consider an independent 
director to be in a monitoring role if he or she serves on not less than two 
of the four main monitoring committees such as audit, governance, 
nomination and compensation. We then extend this definition to lead 
independent monitoring directors (LMONT), which captures the per-
centage of these directors to the number of monitoring directors. As in 
Faleye et al. (2011), we capture advisory directors as those who do not 
serve on any of the monitoring committees. In light of this, we measure 
lead independent advisory directors (LADV) as a percentage of these
directors to the number of advisory directors that serve on committees 
other than audit, governance, nomination and compensation.
3.4. The measure of lead independent director with financial expertise
We capture lead independent directors with financial expertise 
(LEADFE) by using the BoardEx North America board and director 
committees file to identify lead independent directors with financial 
expertise. LEADFE is coded 1 if a lead independent director committee 
role name has “Financial Expert”, and 0 otherwise.
4. Empirical analysis
4.1. Descriptive statistics
Table 1 summarises the descriptive statistics of the main variables 
employed in this study. While the mean percentage of monitoring lead 
independent directors (LMONT) is around 9% with a maximum value of 
33, the advisory lead independent directors represent the average value 
of around 4 with the highest value of 20. The mean discretionary ac-
cruals (DA) in our sample is around 0.092. Table 2 also presents the 
Pearson correlation between the dependent and independent variables 
used in the main analysis. The results show that the correlation between 
DA and LMONT is negative and a positive association between DA and 
LADV, indicating that monitoring lead independent directors have the 
propensity to restrain managerial opportunism. Nevertheless, these are 
preliminary results without the inclusion of control variables and 
therefore inferences can be drawn after the inclusion of the control 
variables in our main regression model. In general, the size of each 
correlation coefficient in Table 2 does not exceed the 0.80 threshold that 
has been documented in the existing literature to trigger any potential 
multicollinearity problems (Hair et al., 2006; Sharma et al., 2017; Ullah 
et al., 2022; Owusu et al., 2024; Ullah et al., 2024).
4.2. Multivariate analysis

ROLE represents monitoring lead independent directors (LMONT) or 
advisory lead independent directors (LADV). Hence, the coefficient β 1

The two additional control variables included in equation (4) are the

Models 1 and 2 of Table 4 show our main results of the effects of 
monitoring and advisory lead independent directors on earnings man-
agement. In Model 1, we regress DA on LMONT and, the coefficient on 
LMONT is − 0.0045 (t-statistics = -3.27). This suggests that a lead in-
dependent director who plays a monitoring role reduces earnings 
management. In model 2, we examine whether a lead independent di-
rector with an advisory role has varying effects on earnings manage-
ment. The coefficient on LADV is negative but not statistically 
significant. We hypothesised that lead independent directors who sit on 
the independent directors-dominated monitoring committees are more 
likely to restrain managerial opportunism than those who sit on the 
executive-dominated advisory committees. Our results in Models 1 and
2 of Table 4 are consistent with our expectation, in that, monitoring lead 
independent directors rather than advisory lead independent directors 
have the propensity to restrain managers from engaging in earnings 
management. Thus, our hypothesis 1 is supported. Our results are also 
economically significant, in that, the coefficient of LMONT in Model 1 of 
Table 4 (0.0045) and the average discretionary accruals in Table 1 
(0.092), lead to a decreasing effect of 4.89% (0.0045/0.092) in earnings 
management.
4.3. Robustness
Thus far, the baseline analysis suggests that monitoring lead inde-
pendent directors rather than advisory lead independent directors’ 
constraint managerial opportunism. We undertake further analysis to 
check the robustness of our baseline results. First, we employ Kothari 
et al. (2005) performance-matched model by including ROA in equation 
(1) to estimate an alternative dependent variable of performance-
matched discretionary accruals (PMDA). The results are contained in 
Models 3 and 4 of Table 4. In Models 3 and 4, LMONT rather than LADV 
is negatively significant with PMDA, implying that our baseline results 
are not sensitive to an alternative measure of earnings management. 
Second, we replace LMONT with the percentage of monitoring lead in-
dependent directors relative to the total number of board members 
(LMONT1) and LADV with the percentage of advisory lead independent 
directors relative to the total number of board members (LADV1) as 
alternative measures and the results are presented in Models 5 and 6 of 
Table 4. Even though the coefficients on LMONT1 and LADV1 have 
become smaller, the results consistently show that LMONT1 rather than 
LADV1 is negative and significantly associated with DA.
Third, accounting literature (e.g., Gunny, 2010; Alhadab & Clacher, 
2018) suggests that previous year earnings management affects a firm’s 
current year earnings manipulative behaviour. For that reason, we 
control the effects of the previous year's earnings management. Table 5 
presents the results. In Model 1, the coefficient on LMONT is negative 
and statistically significant at the 1% level, whilst in Model 2, the co-
efficient on LADV is negative but not statistically significant. These re-
sults show that our baseline results are not sensitive to the previous year 
earnings management.
Prior research (i.e., Faleye et al., 2011) suggests that monitoring 
(advising) quality improves (weakens) when the majority of indepen-
dent directors serve on monitoring committees. In our fourth sensitivity 
test, we group our sample by the size (i.e., high vs low) of independent
directors that serve on monitoring (advisory) committees using the 
median of MONTSIZE (ADVSIZE) and the results are reported in Table 6. 
As Models 1 and 3 of Table 6 show, the results continue to be negative 
and statistically significant at the 5% level or higher but it is more 
pronounced in firms with low intensive monitoring independent di-
rectors. In a similar vein, the results in Models 2 and 4 of Table 6 
continue to be negative but not statistically significant. Therefore, we 
conclude that our baseline results are not sensitive to intensive moni-
toring/advisory independent directors.
4.4. Addressing endogeneity
It is well documented in the empirical accounting literature that 
studies like ours may suffer from endogeneity concerns due to self-
selection bias, reverse causality and omitted variables bias (Larcker & 
Rusticus, 2010; Gippel et al., 2015). For these reasons, the conclusions 
from our baseline analysis may be misleading. We, thus, address the 
endogeneity concerns by implementing different research designs 
including the propensity score matching (PSM), Heckman two-step and 
two-stage least squares (2SLS) that have been widely used in the existing 
literature.
First, we use the PSM method to mitigate the concerns about po-
tential endogeneity issues. We first compare the earnings management 
practices between a group of firms with lead independent directors that 
have a monitoring role (LMONT) and a matched group with lead inde-
pendent directors that do not have a monitoring role on the boards. In 
common with Hsu et al. (2024), we use the nearest neighbour matching 
approach with replacement to match each firm with LMONT and 
without LMONT. Similarly, we repeat the PSM procedures for firms with 
lead independent directors that have an advisory role (LADV) and match 
them with the nearest-neighbour firm without LADV. We then re-
estimate equation (4) using the matched sample firms and report the 
results in Table 7. As Models 1 and 2 of Table 7 indicate, while the co-
efficient on LMONT continues to be negatively and significantly related 
to discretionary accruals, the coefficient on LADV is negative but not 
statistically significant. These findings support the conclusions drawn 
from our baseline analysis and appear not to be affected by endogeneity 
issues.
Second, according to Antonakis et al. (2010) and Larcker and Rus-
ticus (2010), an alternative research design is needed to address endo-
geneity when undertaking a study of this nature. We, therefore, employ 
an alternative two-step model suggested by Heckman (1976) to address 
endogeneity issues. First, we run a probit regression to check the pos-
sibility of firms appointing a monitoring or an advisory lead indepen-
dent director. Specifically, we include the industry average of the 
percentage of lead independent directors (IndPctLead) in addition to all 
the control variables in the probit model in the first step. We then use the 
estimation from the first step to compute the inverse Mills ratio (MILLS) 
and include it in the second step as a control variable in equation (4). 
Our reported results from Models 3 and 4 of Table 7 support our baseline 
analysis, indicating that the conclusions drawn from our baseline anal-
ysis appear not to be affected by endogeneity issues.
Finally, to address any endogeneity concerns that may arise due to 
reverse causality, we employ the 2SLS instrumental variables method. 
Specifically, we use IndPctLead in the first stage as our instrumental 
variable. Theoretically, this instrument has the propensity to influence 
the appointment of a monitoring or an advisory lead independent di-
rector, but it is less likely to affect managerial opportunism (DA). In the 
second stage, we use the predicted LMONT and LADV to explain 
managerial opportunism. As Model 1 of Table 8 indicate, LMONT con-
tinues to be negative and significantly related to DA. In Model 2 of 
Table 8, LADV is still negative and not significantly related to DA. We 
also follow Larcker and Rusticus (2010) and undertake the Durbin-Wu-
Hausman test for endogeneity but all the p-values from the regressions 
in both Models 1 and 2 of Table 8 are higher than 10% significance level, 
supporting the null hypothesis of no endogeneity. These results show

4.5. Monitoring versus advisory lead independent directors and earnings 
persistence
In this section, we examine the consequences of earnings persistence 
around monitoring and advisory lead independent directors. While our 
main results suggest that monitoring rather than advisory lead inde-
pendent directors are associated with lower earnings management, Hsu 
and Hu (2016) argued and provided evidence to show that firms with 
independent directors in advisory roles rather than monitoring roles are 
associated with more earnings persistence and higher future cash flows. 
This contrasting evidence suggests that monitoring and advisory lead 
independent directors may play complementary roles when it comes to 
the overall corporate performance. As an additional test, we follow 
previous literature (Barth et al., 2001; Sloan, 1996; Richardson et al.,
2005; Hsu & Hu, 2016) and utilise earnings persistence measures 
including, (1) earnings (Earn) defined as net income before extraordi-
nary items scaled by average total assets, (2) cash flows (CFO) defined as 
cash flows from operating activities scaled by average total assets, and 
(3) accruals (ACC) defined as the difference between net income before 
extraordinary items and cash flows from operating activities scaled by 
average total assets to proxy earnings quality.

variables. As Panel A shows, the coefficient on LMONT × Earn is only 
significant in Model 5 at the 10% level and is not significant in all other 
models. However, Models 1 to 6 of Panel B reveal that the coefficients on

independent directors, our results in Models 1 to 6 in Panel C show that 
the coefficients on LADV × Earn rather than LMONT × Earn continue to 
be positive and statistically significant, confirming our earlier results 
that monitoring (advisory) lead independent directors are less (more) 
likely to improve earnings persistence and future cash flows.
In Table 10, we decompose earnings to capture cash flows and ac-
cruals components, and the results are detailed in Panels A and B for 
monitoring intensity and advisory intensity, respectively. While the 
coefficients on LMONT × CFO and LMONT × ACC are not significant in 
Panel A except for Model 1 where the coefficient on LMONT × CFO is 
only significant at the 10% level, the coefficients on LADV × CFO and 
LADV × ACC are consistently positive and significant at the 5% level or 
higher in Panel B. We unveil that both cash flows and current accruals 
components consistently improve future earnings in firms with advisory 
lead independent directors. These results imply that both regular inde-
pendent directors’ monitoring and advisory role measures used in Hsu
and Hu (2016) and the lead independent directors’ monitoring and 
advisory role measures utilised in this study provide insights and deepen 
our understanding of the earnings quality effect of board monitoring and 
advisory roles.
Taken together, our findings highlight that while lead independent 
directors with monitoring rather than advisory roles have the propensity 
to reduce abnormal accruals, those in advisory rather than monitoring 
roles improve earnings persistence and future cash flows. Therefore, we 
can conclude that both lead independent directors’ monitoring and 
advisory roles contribute to corporate performance in complementary 
ways.

intensified, policymakers and investors have the incentive to know the 
role play by lead independent directors in managerial opportunism. So 
far, extant literature has focused on the effect of the mere participation 
of lead independent directors on managerial opportunism, paying less 
attention to how lead independent directors’ roles within boardrooms 
may affect managerial opportunism, notably, their monitoring and

Our analysis provides strong evidence that monitoring lead inde-
pendent directors rather than advisory lead independent directors are 
negatively associated with discretionary accruals. We also find that 
advisory rather than monitoring lead independent directors improve 
earnings persistence and future cash flows. When we interact moni-
toring or advisory lead independent directors with financial expertise, 
our results show that, regardless of the role that they play, monitoring 
and advisory lead independent directors have a decreasing effect on 
managerial opportunism. Our results are robust to an alternative mea-
sure of earnings management, monitoring and advisory lead indepen-
dent directors, and alternative research designs including PSM, 
Heckman two-step model, and 2SLS regression model.
Our findings make several contributions to the existing literature. 
First, previous literature looks at the impact of the mere presence of lead 
independent directors on firms’ outcomes, and not much attention has 
been given to their role within corporate boardrooms. Considering that a 
lead independent director policy was introduced in the US to avoid the 
separation of the CEO and the board chair positions, we examine the role
(monitoring and advisory) that lead independent directors can play to 
reduce managerial opportunism. Second, we expand the existing liter-
ature that focuses on monitoring and advisory roles of regular inde-
pendent directors in earnings quality/persistence. We show that lead 
independent directors’ monitoring and advisory roles contribute to 
corporate performance in complementary ways. Finally, our findings 
have important policy implications. Given that monitoring and advisory 
lead independent directors play complementary roles, it is important for 
firms to appoint a lead independent director into a monitoring role to 
ensure strong oversight, accountability and compliance, especially when 
it comes to management discipline around the quality of financial 
report, while in the areas of strategic insight and persistent earnings, an 
advisory lead independent director is suitable.

from the US where a lead independent director policy was introduced to 
avoid the separation of the CEO and board chair positions. For this

reason, we caution against generalising our results to countries with 
different corporate governance environments. We would encourage 
future research to be undertaken in countries such as the UK where the 
combined role of the CEO and the board chair is not permitted but the 
lead independent director policy has been implemented with a different 
rationale. In addition, this study relied on larger firms that have the 
resources to appoint lead independent directors relative to the smaller 
firms. Therefore, caution should be exercised when generalising our 
results to smaller companies in the US or other jurisdictions. Moreover, 
our measure of managerial opportunism is based on discretionary ac-
cruals, as we are interested in the monitoring or advisory role of the lead
independent director around financial reporting decisions that violate 
the underlying accounting principles. Nevertheless, future research 
could also consider real activities earnings management that relates to 
opportunistic business decisions.

Appendix. Variable definitions
.
Definitions
Dependent variable 
DA
Modified Jones discretionary accruals estimated using Dechow et al. (1995) model.
Independent variables 
LEAD
Dummy variable code 1 if a firm has a lead independent director on the board, and 0 otherwise. 
LMONT
Percentage of lead independent monitoring directors to the number of independent monitoring directors. 
LADV
Percentage of lead independent advisory directors to the number of independent advisory directors. 
Moderation variable 
LEADFE
Dummy variable coded 1 if a lead independent committee role name has financial expert, and 0 otherwise.
Corporate governance variables
BDSIZE
The total number of directors.
PctIndDir 
Percentage of independent directors to the total number of directors.
PctFemDir 
Percentage of female directors to the total number of directors.
Duality
Dummy variable coded 1 if the CEO is the chairman positions are occupied by one individual, and 0 otherwise. 
Big4
Dummy variable coded 1 if the firm is audited by one of the big four audit firms, and zero otherwise. 
FINEXP 
Percentage of board of directors with financial expertise to the total number of directors.
MONTSIZE 
Total number of independent monitoring directors.
ADVSIZE
Total number of independent advisory directors.
Control variables 
MBV
The proportion of firms’ market value to the book value of common equity
SIZE
The natural logarithm of a firm’s total asset.
LEV
The total debt to total asset.
OCF
Cash flows from operations to total assets
ROA
Net income before extraordinary items divided by total asset
SALESG
The change in the total sales of the firm
LOSS
Dummy variable coded 1 if net income before extraordinary items is a loss, and zero otherwise.
